spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was part of a strong season for the Captain — 1998 was, of course, the year the Yankees went 114-48 and won the World Series.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: Mark McGwire hit **70 home runs** in 1998, setting what was then the all-time single-season record, breaking Roger Maris's mark of 61 set in 1961. It was one of the most celebrated chases in baseball history, with McGwire and Sammy Sosa (who finished with 66) captivating the country all summer long.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here are the best left-handed hitters against left-handed pitchers in 1998 (min. 60 AB), ranked by OBP:

| Rank | Player | AB | AVG | OBP | SLG | HR |
|------|--------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | **John Olerud** | 152 | .375 | .463 | .513 | 5 |
| 2 | **Barry Bonds** | 167 | .281 | .417 | .497 | 9 |
| 3 | **Larry Walker** | 131 | .321 | .411 | .511 | 3 |
| 4 | **Rusty Greer** | 175 | .337 | .402 | .451 | 4 |
| 5 | **Andy Fox** | 96 | .333 | .390 | .427 | 0 |
| 6 | **Mo Vaughn** | 198 | .333 | .386 | .586 | 14 |
| 7 | **Bobby Abreu** | 103 | .320 | .386 | .408 | 0 |
| 8 | **Will Clark** | 147 | .327 | .385 | .517 | 5 |
| 9 | **Rafael Palmeiro** | 230 | .317 | .379 | .591 | 15 |
| 10 | **Jim Thome** | 142 | .289 | .377 | .500 | 7 |

**Highlights:**

- 🏆 **John Olerud** was in a class by himself — .375/.463/.513 against lefties is absurd. He was famously one of the most disciplined contact hitters of his era, and this shows it.
- **Barry Bonds** was Barry Bonds — his OBP (.417) was inflated by 39 walks in 167 AB against lefties. Opposing managers walked him even when their pitcher was the "advantaged" side.
- **Mo Vaughn** and **Rafael Palmeiro** were the power leaders among the group, combining for 29 HR and slugging .586/.591 respectively — both massive power numbers in the typically tough lefty-on-lefty matchup.
- **Ken Griffey Jr.** didn't crack the top 10 by OBP, but his **.701 SLG** (21 HR in 177 AB!) against lefties was arguably the most dominant raw power performance on the list.

The conventional wisdom that lefties struggle against same-handed pitchers clearly didn't apply to these guys in 1998.

tool_input.query:
SELECT
    p.batter,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::float / NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1), 0) AS avg,
    (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::float /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1) + SUM(p.walk), 0) AS obp,
    (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::float /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1), 0) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND p.bathand = 'L'
  AND p.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, player_name
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1) >= 60
ORDER BY obp DESC
LIMIT 25;

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent | Venue |
|------|--------|------|----------|-------|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland | @ Chicago White Sox | Away |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado | vs. Texas | Home |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado | vs. St. Louis | Home |

A couple of fun notes:
- Colorado hosted **two** of the three cycles in '98 — not a shock given Coors Field's run-friendly environment, which helps hitters rack up extra-base hits.
- Bichette and Perez hit their cycles just six weeks apart at the same ballpark.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.gid,
    p.batter,
    pl.usename,
    pl.lastname,
    g.date,
    g.visteam,
    g.hometeam,
    p.batteam,
    SUM(p.single) AS singles,
    SUM(p.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(p.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hrs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON p.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.gid, p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname, g.date, g.visteam, g.hometeam, p.batteam
HAVING SUM(p.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(p.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date;

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text: The 1998 Yankees went **114-48** in the regular season — one of the best records in baseball history. That .704 winning percentage powered them to the AL East title, and they went on to sweep through the postseason for the World Series championship. A truly legendary team.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text:
There it is — **714 regular-season home runs**, which is the canonical career total everyone knows.

Breaking it all down:
- **Regular season:** 714 HR
- **World Series:** 15 HR
- **All-Star Game:** 1 HR

The 714 regular-season figure stood as the all-time MLB record for nearly four decades until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974. Ruth's 15 World Series homers are also the most in postseason history.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'

SELECT g.gametype, SUM(p.hr) AS hr_total
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'ruthb101'
GROUP BY g.gametype
ORDER BY g.gametype

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text: Hank Aaron hit **755 career regular-season home runs** — a record that stood for 33 years until Barry Bonds surpassed it in 2007. Aaron slugged his way to that total over 23 seasons (1954–1976), with remarkable consistency rather than a single peak — he never hit more than 47 in a season, but he just never stopped hitting them.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'aaroh101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

spec.question: How many home runs did Barry Bonds hit in 2001?

questions.response_text: Barry Bonds hit **73 home runs** in 2001 — the all-time single-season record. He shattered Mark McGwire's previous record of 70 (set in 1998), and that mark still stands today.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays
JOIN retro.games ON plays.gid = games.gid
WHERE plays.batter = 'bondb001'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM games.date) = 2001
  AND games.gametype = 'regular'

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: **262 hits** — Ichiro's record-breaking 2004 season, which surpassed George Sisler's 84-year-old single-season hits record of 257 (set in 1920). It remains the MLB record for hits in a single season to this day.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
That's a fantastic question, but I have to be upfront: **WAR (Wins Above Replacement) is not available in the Retrosheet database.** Retrosheet contains play-by-play and box score data, but WAR is a calculated metric from Baseball Reference (rWAR) or FanGraphs (fWAR) that isn't part of this corpus. Trade history (who was swapped for whom) also isn't tracked in Retrosheet.

So I genuinely can't answer this one from the data I have access to — it would require either a WAR database or a trades database, neither of which is here.

---

**What I *can* offer from training knowledge (for framing, not hard numbers):**

The most commonly cited lopsided trades in baseball history include:

- **Nolan Ryan for Jim Fregosi (1971, Mets → Angels)** — Ryan went on to become arguably the greatest strikeout pitcher ever; Fregosi lasted two forgettable seasons in New York.
- **Lou Brock for Ernie Broglio (1964, Cubs → Cardinals)** — Brock became a Hall of Fame base-stealer; Broglio won 7 games the rest of his career.
- **Frank Robinson (1966, Reds → Orioles)** — dealt as an "old 30," Robinson promptly won the Triple Crown and a World Series MVP.
- **Jeff Bagwell for Larry Andersen (1990, Red Sox → Astros)** — a rental reliever for a Hall of Fame first baseman.

For precise WAR comparisons, **Baseball Reference's Trade Tracker** (baseball-reference.com) is the right tool — they've done exactly this analysis with their rWAR data.

Want me to dig into any of these specific trades using what *is* in Retrosheet — like career stat comparisons for the players involved?

checks.name: decline_signal
checks.detail: decline-signal phrase(s) present: ['is not available', "isn't part of"]
